raw_requirement: Generate application to greet user with proper time like Good Morning, Evening. And ask if he/she want to see Quote of the Day or Joke of the Day. And Reply.
normalised_requirement:
Build a web application that:
1. Detects or accepts the user's local time.
2. Greets the user with the appropriate time-of-day salutation (Good Morning / Good Afternoon / Good Evening / Good Night).
3. Prompts the user to choose between 'Quote of the Day' and 'Joke of the Day'.
4. Fetches and displays the selected content from an appropriate third-party API.
5. Handles API errors gracefully with a retry option.
gherkin_business:
Feature: Time-based greeting and daily content selection

  Background:
    Given the user has the application open
    And the application has detected the client device local time

  Scenario Outline: Greeting displayed based on time of day
    Given the local time is <time>
    When the application loads
    Then the greeting "<greeting>" is displayed to the user

    Examples:
      | time  | greeting          |
      | 06:00 | Good Morning      |
      | 13:00 | Good Afternoon    |
      | 19:00 | Good Evening      |
      | 23:00 | Good Night        |

  Scenario: User is prompted to select content after greeting
    Given the greeting has been displayed
    When the user views the interface
    Then the options "Quote of the Day" and "Joke of the Day" are presented

  Scenario: User receives a quote after selecting Quote of the Day
    Given the greeting and content options are displayed
    When the user selects "Quote of the Day"
    Then the application retrieves a quote from the Quote-of-the-Day API
    And the retrieved quote text is displayed in the same session

  Scenario: User receives a joke after selecting Joke of the Day
    Given the greeting and content options are displayed
    When the user selects "Joke of the Day"
    Then the application retrieves a joke from the Joke API
    And the retrieved joke text is displayed in the same session

  Scenario: API failure shows error and retry option
    Given the user has selected a content option
    When the corresponding API call fails
    Then a user-readable error message is displayed
    And the user is offered the option to retry the request

  Scenario: Retry after API failure retrieves and displays content
    Given an API failure message and retry option are shown
    When the user selects retry
    Then the application calls the API again
    And the retrieved content is displayed upon success

  Scenario Outline: Greeting displayed based on time of day

  Scenario: Retry after API failure retrieves and displays content

environment_options.rationale: The requirement for client-device local time detection, a single-session interactive UI with two user-selectable options, third-party API calls, and inline error-with-retry handling maps directly to a React component tree where time is read via the browser's Date API and API calls are made client-side or via lightweight server routes, all of which Next.js handles natively with minimal infrastructure.

The requirement's emphasis on a simple two-option prompt, single text response display, and retry flow within the same interface session is achievable with a thin Express server rendering HTML fragments via HTMX, keeping the stack lightweight given there is no stated need for complex client-side state.

FastAPI with Jinja2 templates can serve the time-based greeting (resolved client-side via a small JS snippet), render the two-option prompt, proxy calls to the Quote-of-the-Day or Joke API, and return user-readable error messages with a retry option, satisfying all stated functional requirements with a widely adopted Python stack.

selected_environment.rationale: The requirement for client-device local time detection, a single-session interactive UI with two user-selectable options, third-party API calls, and inline error-with-retry handling maps directly to a React component tree where time is read via the browser's Date API and API calls are made client-side or via lightweight server routes, all of which Next.js handles natively with minimal infrastructure.
